posts.caption:
At Chanel’s Fall/Winter 2026 Haute Couture show today, Matthieu Blazy took the fairy tale all the way down to the shoes.
Under a set built around a giant beanstalk (Jack and the Beanstalk, obviously), the collection leaned into storybook logic throughout — looks referencing Puss in Boots and Goldilocks, a jacket lined with illustrated pages, mock Post-it notes embroidered into silk. But the shoes are where the references get literal: heels sculpted into butterflies, others shaped like golden eggs, small enough to miss on a first look.
“Chanel couture is not about the big wow,” Blazy said backstage. “It’s more like a miniature — something that feels precious, but that you can also put in your pocket.”
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 Vogue Runway
#Chanel #MatthieuBlazy #HauteCouture #A2ZFashionMagazine
posts.alt_text:
At @jonathan.anderson’s latest Haute Couture show for @dior, the accessories did more than complete the looks. They carried the collection’s ideas in miniature. 

One of the season’s key references was the work of Lynda Benglis, whose sculptural practice is rooted in material, gesture and form. That influence came through in the pleated metallic headpieces, dramatic hand-held pieces, shoes, and reimagined Lady Dior icons, which felt almost like wearable sculptures. 

Another thread came from Indian craft, with Anderson looking to the traditions of chintz and gemstone carving. On the porcelain-like clutches, delicate floral embroidery recalled the decorative language of historic chintz textiles.

In the end, it was the details that made the collection feel truly couture. Swipe to see the accessories!

Words by Josephine Jiang

Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.

📷 @dior, @jonathananderson, Vogue Runway

#dior #hautecouture #accessory #jonathananderson

posts.caption:
Jonathan Anderson’s second haute couture collection for Dior, shown yesterday at the Musée Rodin in Paris, is built entirely around the work of one artist: American sculptor Lynda Benglis.
Dior’s own show notes framed it directly — Benglis’ work takes two-dimensional materials and transforms them into three-dimensional form through knotting, pleating, or moulding. Anderson built the entire collection as couture’s answer to that same process.
Some of the translations are almost exact. Her pleated metallic sculpture “Goliath” became the silver lamé dress draped into a bow. Her 1979 work “Zanzidae: Peacock Series” — a fan-shaped piece covered in appliqué flowers and beading, inspired by birds she saw while staying in Ahmedabad, India — became the embroidered fan dress, reportedly worn as looks 24 and 30 in the show. Her “Sparkle Knots,” made from shimmering paper stretched over chicken wire, reappear as the netted, jewel-toned skirts. And her poured latex and wax sculptures, arguably her most famous body of work from the 1970s, run through the collection as the dripping, molten metallic textures on multiple looks.
Speaking about Lynda Benglis ahead of this show, Jonathan called her one of the greatest living artists working today.
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 Vogue Runway, Lynda Benglis
#Dior #LyndaBenglis #HauteCouture #JonathanAnderson #A2ZFashionMagazine

posts.caption:
On 7 July, @chanelofficial’s AW26 Couture show by @Matthieu_Blazy took place at the Grand Palais during Haute Couture Week. As his second couture collection for the house, Blazy turned the runway into a fairytale-inspired setting filled with sprawling vines, oversized vases, and abundant flowers.
The botanical world of the set was reflected throughout the garments. Vibrant floral motifs, intricate vine details, and lush botanical elements were brought to life through Chanel’s heritage materials, including organza, mesh, and tweed, reflecting the house’s exceptional couture savoir-faire. Alongside these natural inspirations, Blazy offered a contemporary reinterpretation of Chanel’s signature tweed, introducing silhouettes such as dropped-waist dresses and skirts, while reimagining the classic tweed weave through a structured grid-like construction. The collection continued his exploration of refined yet practical silhouettes that follow the natural shape of the body with ease and elegance.
Blazy’s fairytale-like yet modern sensibility found a natural dialogue with Chanel’s heritage. By approaching the house’s classic codes through his own perspective, he preserved the essence of Chanel while giving it a renewed expression that feels familiar yet relevant across generations. Swipe right to see more looks from the collection.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now
📷 Isidore Montag (Gorunway.com)
#CHANEL #MatthieuBlazy
#HauteCouture #AW26

posts.caption:
As a huge Antoni Gaudí fan, seeing Barcelona’s buildings run through @schiaparelli Fall 2026 Couture felt especially fascinating, not as a literal postcard from the city, but as architecture rebuilt on the body.
The shimmering trencadís façade of Casa Batlló, where broken ceramic and glass turn a building into light, was translated into tiny flecks across dresses. The sun-like mosaic medallions on the ceiling of Park Güell’s Hypostyle Room reappeared in a dense, radiating top. And the painted main staircase of Casa Milà, where Aleix Clapés’ dreamlike mural decoration, was echoed in glossy, melting folds.
In the show notes, @danielroseberry described the creative formula he thought would lead him there: Step 1: go on a trip. Step 2: visit an iconic architectural site and have a transcendent experience. Step 3: come home inspired and begin. For this season, that meant travelling to Barcelona to see Gaudí’s work.
But the formula did not work. The more he tried to force inspiration into a method, the more blocked he became. Only when certainty slipped away did the collection begin to find its own shape.
Swipe to see the architectural inspirations behind this season’s collection.
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @architecturehub_, @arte.antiqua, Vogue Runway, F. Delventhal(Flickr)
#Schiaparelli #DanielRoseberry #HauteCouture #Gaudi #Architecture

posts.caption:
On 6 July, @Schiaparelli by @danielroseberry presented its AW26 Haute Couture collection in Paris, and just hours later, @zendaya appeared in the collection’s closing look at the world premiere of #TheOdyssey in London.
The closing look features a sculptural bustier crafted in silicone, paired with a gradient fringe detail transitioning from white to a mirrored finish. The design creates the illusion of a dress that glows from within, showcasing Schiaparelli’s signature approach to craftsmanship and surreal elegance.
For this special moment, Zendaya’s stylist @luxurylaw took the look straight from the runway and traveled to London immediately afterward. A remarkable fashion moment shaped by the collaboration between #Schiaparelli, #Zendaya, and her stylist #LawRoach.
Swipe right to discover every detail.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now
📷 @Schiaparelli, @luxurylaw, @ly.as

posts.caption:
From #TheGreatGatsby’s Jordan Baker to Diana, Princess of Wales, #ElizabethDebicki has inhabited some of popular culture’s most recognisable figures.
In our July cover story, the award-winning actor reflects on finding the humanity behind iconic characters and why every role begins with a blank slate. “But personally, I’m interested in trying to shed that and create as much open space to imagine, as you would with a character that’s entirely your own creation.”
Discover the full interview at the link in our bio.
The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.
Debicki wears @Cartier throughout.
Words @tessdevivie
Photography @justinridler
Dp @alicenaish
Styling @jessicasteuarthoyler

Makeup @victoriabaron

posts.alt_text:
For #ElizabethDebicki, coming home to Melbourne is always a gift. As our July cover star, the award-winning actor reflects on family, finding meaning in the stories she tells, and why following instinct has become the guiding force of her career.

“As an Australian, we have this unspoken pact that you do not get to hate planes, flights or turbulence if you want to go back home and see the people you love.”

The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.
Debicki wears @Cartier throughout.

posts.caption:
Elizabeth Debicki has become one of the most compelling actors of her generation. From Melbourne to Hollywood, our July cover star reflects on inhabiting iconic women, choosing stories with purpose, and why she’s only just getting started.
“My vetting process when I read a script is asking myself, if someone called me up and said you have to go on set next week, how excited am I about doing that?”
The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.
#ElizabethDebicki wears Panthère de @Cartier necklace in white gold, onyx, set with four emeralds and 1,478 brilliant-cut diamonds, and Panthère de Cartier ring in white gold, set with four emeralds, onyx and 298 brilliant-cut diamonds. @toni_maticevski dress, bodysuit and skirt.

posts.caption:
For his debut @boloria show, the materials were fine and the silhouettes elegant and vaporous – there was a hazy sense of period, a little 1940s, a little 1970s, a touch of the 19th century. @oliviertheyskens has long dealt in the achingly gorgeous. ⁠
⁠
Read @alexanderfury's review at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Boloria Spring/Summer 2027
posts.alt_text:
@matthieu_blazy’s @chanelofficial haute couture collection began with fairy tales and sprouting beans, translating Gabrielle Chanel’s life and apartment into an overgrown world of vine-like dresses, embroidered to-do lists and a final little black revenge dress.

Here, @alexanderfury lists five things to know about the collection ➡️

posts.caption: This isn’t Matthew’s strongest collection. While I really appreciated the craftsmanship and the intricate embroidery, the proportions felt off in several looks. A few silhouettes even had a scarecrow-like quality, which made them feel more costume than couture. Beautiful techniques, but not the level of refinement and elegance I expect from Chanel Haute Couture.

posts.caption: Thank you for your service to all who serve in the pursuit of liberty and justice for all. For all who stand to protect the rights and freedoms of the good citizens of the world. It is with great sorrow and imagining that the USA in the current state of crisis that it is in for most of you who fought and fight for her would be greatly disappointed and likely choose not to do so now. Sorry we're letting the debt of liberty your service provides down. To the remaining good citizens of the world who wish for love, peace, prosperity, joy, and happiness for others of which the numbers continue to dwindle there are many still in the USA. Many that still wish for a better life for others and a better world than how they found it with a bright future. Look forward. Steady your gaze on your heart and dreams. Find your common humanity with courage and kindness and share it.

posts.caption:
With @arca1000000 on the runway and @another_man editor-in-chief @elliegracecumming styling the show, @gmbh_official’s tenth-anniversary collection is a meditation on Berlin itself, from its 1920s couture salons, forgotten Jewish designers and the fashion history fractured by the Nazi regime.
Designers @benjaminhuseby and @serhatisik__ folded those references into the brand’s own archetypes, from silvered sportswear to couture-inflected tailoring, asking how a collection might hold memory, resistance and the demand to be seen, heard, felt and loved.

posts.caption:
At @berlinfashionwe, @johnlawrencesullivan_official shows its Spring/Summer 2027 collection, Androgyny, presented during @referencestudios’ Intervention programme.
Styled by @another_man’s @jordanduddy, the collection draws inspiration from Bettina Rheims’ 1990 portrait series Modern Lovers, and fuses its liberated notions of gender with the Japanese concept of shibari.

posts.caption:
Few artists manage to capture how it feels to live inside the modern internet with such fidelity to the real, dizzying experience as @jonrafman. ⁠
⁠
In the 2025 video and installation Main Stream Media Network, the Canadian artist displayed music, images and music videos from a cast of imaginary artists – including Cloudy Heart, Pledged Torment, Iron Tears, and Flux Arcana – working in niche and newly invented genres, and realised with the assistance of AI. These characters and their creations were aired via the titular MSM Network, in a “nostalgic” throwback to the collective viewing experiences of years gone by (think: hanging out and watching MTV together).⁠
⁠
While tools like game engines and AI have “liberated” the artist to create and distribute new kinds of work – and forced him to keep moving – the MSM universe has also been enabled to evolve organically, without a clear centre. This is epitomised in a new book conceived alongside @happypumpkin108 (known for co-founding the clothing brand Farmer’s Daughter) and architect @liam.denhamer. Their collaboration, MSM Vol 2, taps into a decentralised network of writers, artists and collectives to expand the universe even further.⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, Rafman explains to @t.s.waite why he made a hardback art book in our new media landscape, how the Disney business model inspired his approach to making art and why people constantly misunderstand how to creatively use AI 📲⁠
⁠
📸 MSM Vol 2. Artwork by #JonRafman

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Geetika Sachdev (@geetikasachdev)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Make-up Artist: Itika Chugh (@fashionmakeupbyitikachugh)

posts.alt_text:
From discussing masculinity and mindset to family, fashion, and finding comfort in authenticity, Jasprit Bumrah (@jaspritb1) keeps it refreshingly real in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved). Speaking about everything from his love for fragrances and watches to switching off with family time, the ace cricketer proves that personal style is less about trends and more about individuality. He also shares why collaborating with Ralph Lauren Fragrances (@ralphlaurenfragrances) felt like a natural fit for his personal style and personality. Whether on the field or off duty, his approach remains grounded, thoughtful and deeply personal.

Tap the link in bio to watch the full video.

Styling: @eshaamiin1 

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)

posts.alt_text:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team. 

Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

Paris Haute Couture Week Fall/Winter 2026-2027 opened with a spectacular display of creativity, where craftsmanship met imagination at every turn. Schiaparelli (@schiaparelli) captivated with Daniel Roseberry (@danielroseberry)’s surreal sculptural creations, Dior (@dior) saw Jonathan Anderson (@jonathan.anderson)’s deepen his artistic vision through nature-inspired couture, Iris van Herpen (@irisvanherpen) pushed the boundaries of innovation with her mesmerising plasma-infused finale, and Rahul Mishra (@rahulmishra_7)’s ‘Devi’ transformed the beauty of ancient Indian temple sculptures into breathtaking wearable art.

Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05) is set to unveil his debut couture collection at Paris Haute Couture Week as a Guest House on the official Fédération de la Haute Couture et de la Mode calendar. Marking the brand’s first couture show on the prestigious calendar, the presentation will spotlight Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotraworld)’s signature dialogue between exceptional Indian craftsmanship and contemporary couture. Alongside the collection, Manish Malhotra Jewellery (@manishmalhotrajewellery) will also unveil an extraordinary High Jewellery presentation featuring one-of-a-kind gemstone creations that extend the collection’s narrative. With the collection, casting, and show concept remaining closely guarded, all eyes are on Paris for this landmark debut.
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia #ManishMalhotra #ManishMalhotraWorld #HauteCoutureWeek2026

Rahul Mishra (@rahulmishra_7) unveiled his Haute Couture Fall/Winter 2026/2027 collection, ‘Devi: The Eternal Muse’, at Paris Haute Couture Week. Inspired by the divine feminine and the timeless beauty of ancient temples, the collection celebrated centuries-old Indian craftsmanship through exquisite couture. Among those in attendance were Isha Ambani and Cardi B (@iamcardib) who turned heads in striking goddess-inspired looks. With intricate embroidery and masterful handwork, Mishra once again brought the artistry of Indian couture to the global stage.
📸: Getty Images
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.alt_text:
Our cover star opts for cinema that sparks conversations, takes pride in her individuality, and fiercely protects her mental peace. “Today, there’s clarity in my choices. I’m shedding old versions of myself to make space for newer, riskier, more interesting ones,” she says, unfiltered, unassuming and refreshingly candid. Bhumi Satish Pednekkar welcomes Brides Today (@bhumisatishpednekkar) into her world of stylish experiments, socially resonant movies, sustainable choices, and the legacy she hopes to leave behind. 

Check out the complete cover story in the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Designer Lutz Huelle (@lutz_huelle_paris) has just signed a long-term licensing agreement with DAL Trading, a family-owned manufacturing company based in Italy’s Marche region, and its promise is a new model for independent designers.
Huelle’s career is an unwitting paradigm for how all the small miracles of fashion were crushed by the wheels of industry. “Felipe Oliveira Baptista, AF Vandervorst, Anne Valérie Hash, Gaspard Yurkievich, Jeremy Scott, that was my generation,” he muses. “The only person who kind of managed to come back now is Haider Ackermann. Otherwise, from my generation, there’s no one left, and it makes me really sad to be honest.”
Tim Blanks (@timblanks) spoke to Huelle about fashion's lost generation—and the deal he hopes will change the script. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @simonhabegger, @lutz_huelle_paris
posts.alt_text:
When @Glossier was founded in 2014, it exerted a kind of gravity, pulling cool girls and cool-girl aspirants into its direct-to-consumer orbit with its Top Shelf interviews and minimalist product ethos. More recently, as the brand has come down from its peak $2 billion valuation, its social currency has been diluted.

Now, the label is attempting to recapture its marketing prowess with a nostalgic New York-centric activation around its hero product Balm Dot Com. 

BoF's Brennan Kilbane (@brennankilbane) spoke with their newly installed chief marketing officer Nicole Solórzano about how the label’s future outlook is grounded in the past. Read more #linkinbio

📷 @glossier

posts.alt_text:
Coty and Kering have reached an agreement to transfer the Gucci licence to L’Oréal, the three companies announced on Tuesday. Coty will receive $400 million for the early termination of the license agreement, which was set to expire in 2028, and to provide it with funds to continue running the business until at least June 30, 2027.

Read the full story by BoF’s @danielakmorosini #linkinbio 

📷 @gucci

posts.caption:
The first dress @Jonathan.Anderson ever made was fashioned from handpainted chestnut leaves. It was for his high school diploma in art. He was remembering it as a patchwork mess while he cast his eye over the chiffon and lace wedding dress, beaded with ferns and embroidered with feathered dandelions, that closed his second haute couture show for Christian @Dior on Monday.
Dior demands a kind of grandeur from its designers. Anderson’s workload is particularly extravagant, and exhaustion is a non-negotiable. Maybe that’s why his latest collection felt like such a pile-on, and also why one of its most striking subtexts was the deliberately rough, unfinished nature of so many of the pieces.
Read Tim Blanks (@timblanks)’ full report on Jonathan Anderson’s sophomore couture collection for Christian Dior at the #linkinbio
📷 @dior, Spotlight/Launchmetrics.com
posts.alt_text:
Designer Lutz Huelle (@lutz_huelle_paris) has just signed a long-term licensing agreement with DAL Trading, a family-owned manufacturing company based in Italy’s Marche region, and its promise is a new model for independent designers.

Huelle’s career is an unwitting paradigm for how all the small miracles of fashion were crushed by the wheels of industry. “Felipe Oliveira Baptista, AF Vandervorst, Anne Valérie Hash, Gaspard Yurkievich, Jeremy Scott, that was my generation,” he muses. “The only person who kind of managed to come back now is Haider Ackermann. Otherwise, from my generation, there’s no one left, and it makes me really sad to be honest.”

Tim Blanks (@timblanks) spoke to Huelle about fashion's lost generation—and the deal he hopes will change the script. Read more #linkinbio

📷 @simonhabegger, @lutz_huelle_paris

posts.caption:
There’s a theme popping up all over fashion’s summer campaigns amid a growing consumer desire for all things analog. From J.Crew’s “Camp Crew” campaign starring a bevy of 2010s supermodels to Rhode’s Newport, Rhode Island pop-up with lobster rolls and basket-adorned bicycles, brands are leaning into symbols associated with what consumers see as a simpler time — whether they remember it or not.
But when so many brands, from luxury houses to accessible beauty brands, use a similar filter, these escapist campaigns can start to feel that they’re tapping into the very corporatisation consumers want to avoid. To survive the sea of sameness and avoid coming off as hollow, brands are connecting digital campaigns to physical spaces to move the summer fantasy beyond the screen and enable consumers to actively engage with the analog ideal they’re projecting.
BoF’s Haley Crawford (@haleycrawf) explores why the best campaigns go beyond the aesthetic. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @jcrew, @setactive, @rhode, @chloe, @mango

posts.caption:
@MonaKattan has been collecting fragrances for most of her life. That obsession eventually became @Kayali — a fragrance brand she built inside Huda Beauty, the global cosmetics company she co-founded with her sister Huda Kattan.
In 2020, something shifted. Mona entered therapy and uncovered a pattern that ran through her entire entrepreneurial journey: she had never built anything entirely on her own. She began to ask herself what it would mean to do something, fully, by herself.
“I am a very collaborative person, but I don’t want to sacrifice my vision,” she says. “Sometimes, in a partnership … having to move both feet in the same direction doesn’t really work if you’re not able to decide on your own. That’s where I realised that if I want Kayali to survive and thrive, I need to create my own path.”
That path was made possible through a complex corporate carve-out that separated Kayali from the Huda Beauty group and brought in General Atlantic in as Kayali’s new backer.
Mona joined BoF founder and CEO @ImranAmed on stage at The Business of Beauty Global Forum in Napa Valley, California, to pull back the curtain on that corporate split and dive deeper into the realities of building a brand within a multi-stakeholder ecosystem.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #BoFPodcast
#BoFBeauty

posts.caption:
I think in this era of AI, it is so, so important that we see work like this. Work that reminds people what actual human creativity can look like. The research, the references, the craftsmanship, the slightly insane ideas, the years of knowledge behind actually being able to execute them. Because this is peak creativity for me. This is peak couture. This is how you open couture week.
Daniel Roseberry has been making magic at Schiaparelli for years now, and I genuinely think this is one of his best collections in the last couple of seasons. His tenure has been so interesting to watch because, yes, he has built such a recognisable visual language for the house, but when he is really good, he also knows how to push it somewhere new. And I think that is exactly what happened here.
Art, architecture, nature, history, the body, Elsa Schiaparelli herself. It is this beautiful coming together of so many things without feeling like references for the sake of references.
Built around the idea of The Call of the Void, the collection embraced uncertainty instead of trying to control it. There is this whole otherworldly, under-the-sea energy running through the collection. The anatomy references are still there, but updated and mutated through latex tentacles, silicone bustiers, fish scales, real flowers, sculpted crinoline and these strange, almost alien textures. And then you have a perfume bottle reimagined as a jacket, Gaudí ceilings translated onto clothes, incredible fringe work, movement, insane construction. So much to love!
Fashion is art. We all know that. But if you ever needed convincing, or even just a reminder, this collection is exactly that.
posts.alt_text: Chanel chanel chanel!!!! We are obsessed 🫶🏻

posts.caption:
Some editorials ask you to look at the clothes. Others ask you to look at the woman wearing them.
For The Face Winter 2025, Irina Shayk steps into a world where glamour feels both cinematic and strangely intimate. Photographed by Drew Vickers, the story strips away the excess often associated with modern fashion imagery, replacing it with sharp contrasts, lingerie-inspired silhouettes, and portraits that rely almost entirely on presence.
Rather than performing for the camera, Shayk allows each image to unfold with remarkable restraint. Every frame feels deliberate, balancing softness with confidence and sensuality with precision. It’s a reminder that the strongest fashion images rarely depend on elaborate sets, they depend on a model who understands exactly how to hold a photograph.
Drew Vickers’ photography amplifies that tension. His clean compositions and carefully controlled lighting create images that feel timeless rather than trend-driven, while Danielle Emerson’s styling references classic lingerie dressing without slipping into nostalgia. The result is an editorial that feels confident, modern, and quietly unforgettable.
Some editorials are remembered for the clothes. Others are remembered for the feeling they leave behind. This is one of them.
Publication: The Face Winter 2025/2026
Model: Irina Shayk
Photography: Drew Vickers
Styling: Danielle Emerson
Hair: Olivier Schawalder
Makeup: Yadim

posts.caption:
Fashion has always loved a great designer-muse relationship. But few have shaped pop culture quite like Donatella Versace and Lady Gaga.
When Lady Gaga became one of the biggest stars in the world, she didn’t just wear Versace, she embodied it.
At a time when Donatella was redefining the house after Gianni Versace’s legacy, Gaga brought the brand into a new cultural conversation. Suddenly, Versace wasn’t just synonymous with ‘90s supermodels and Italian glamour; it became the uniform of pop’s boldest provocateur.
Their relationship went far beyond celebrity dressing. Gaga regularly wore custom Versace on stage, at red carpets, and in editorials, while openly celebrating Donatella as both a designer and a friend. In return, Donatella embraced Gaga as one of the house’s defining muses. The bond became so close that Gaga even dedicated the song Donatella on ARTPOP to her which was transforming the designer into a genuine pop culture icon in her own right.
It’s difficult to imagine the 2010s without the two of them. Together, they helped redefine what Versace represented for a new generation. The brand became louder, bolder, unapologetically glamorous, and deeply connected to pop music and queer culture. While Versace had always been admired within fashion, its relationship with Lady Gaga introduced the house to an entirely new audience that embraced Donatella as more than a designer - she became a personality, a meme, an icon, and, for many, the glamorous mother figure of fashion.
Of course, Donatella Versace’s influence was never built by one person alone. She had already spent years shaping the house and proving herself as a designer. But Lady Gaga undeniably amplified that story. Their friendship blurred the lines between fashion and music in a way that felt authentic, creating one of the defining cultural partnerships of the decade.
Some collaborations sell clothes. This one built a legacy.
posts.alt_text:
Some editorials ask you to look at the clothes. Others ask you to look at the woman wearing them.

For The Face Winter 2025, Irina Shayk steps into a world where glamour feels both cinematic and strangely intimate. Photographed by Drew Vickers, the story strips away the excess often associated with modern fashion imagery, replacing it with sharp contrasts, lingerie-inspired silhouettes, and portraits that rely almost entirely on presence.

Rather than performing for the camera, Shayk allows each image to unfold with remarkable restraint. Every frame feels deliberate, balancing softness with confidence and sensuality with precision. It’s a reminder that the strongest fashion images rarely depend on elaborate sets, they depend on a model who understands exactly how to hold a photograph.

Drew Vickers’ photography amplifies that tension. His clean compositions and carefully controlled lighting create images that feel timeless rather than trend-driven, while Danielle Emerson’s styling references classic lingerie dressing without slipping into nostalgia. The result is an editorial that feels confident, modern, and quietly unforgettable.

Some editorials are remembered for the clothes. Others are remembered for the feeling they leave behind. This is one of them.

Credits

Publication: The Face Winter 2025/2026
Model: Irina Shayk
Photography: Drew Vickers
Styling: Danielle Emerson
Hair: Olivier Schawalder
Makeup: Yadim

posts.caption:
Few film campaigns have become as instantly recognizable as Lars von Trier’s Orgasm Portraits for Nymphomaniac.
Released in 2013 ahead of the film’s premiere, the series abandoned traditional movie posters in favor of something radically simple: close-up portraits of every major cast member at the exact moment of orgasm. There were no elaborate sets or dramatic costumes—just facial expressions, stripped of context and impossible to ignore.
The images quickly became cultural talking points. Some viewed them as pure provocation, others as dark comedy, while many saw them as an extension of von Trier’s long-standing fascination with vulnerability, intimacy, and the emotional extremes of human experience. By isolating a single expression, the portraits blurred the line between performance and reality, leaving viewers to question what they were actually looking at.
More than a decade later, the series remains one of cinema’s most memorable visual campaigns. Not because it relied on explicit imagery, but because it proved that a single facial expression could generate more conversation than almost any traditional film poster.
Sometimes the most provocative image is also the simplest.
Photography: Casper Sejersen
Creative Direction: Lars von Trier
Film: Nymphomaniac (2013)
Cast: Charlotte Gainsbourg, Stacy Martin, Shia LaBeouf, Uma Thurman, Willem Dafoe, Stellan Skarsgård, Jamie Bell, Mia Goth, Christian Slater, Connie Nielsen, Udo Kier, Sophie Kennedy Clark, Nicolas Bro, Jens Albinus.

posts.caption:
When Justice emerged in the late 2000s, they did more than reinvent French electro, they created an aesthetic that shaped an entire generation. Black leather jackets, distressed denim, stark graphics, and their iconic cross logo became part of a visual language that felt as influential as their music.
With † (Cross), the duo fused distorted sound with a raw, cinematic identity that blurred the lines between fashion, art, and nightlife. Their world was dark, effortless, and unmistakably cool, inspiring everyone from club kids to luxury designers.
Unlike trend-driven artists, Justice built a timeless image rooted in consistency rather than reinvention. Their influence still echoes through fashion editorials, runway collections, and contemporary streetwear, proving that true style doesn’t follow trends—it creates them.
Justice didn’t just soundtrack a generation. They dressed it.

posts.caption:
Before millions discovered her through America’s Next Top Model, Allison Harvard had built a cult following on the internet. Her striking features, oversized eyes, vintage-inspired styling, and dreamlike photographs made her one of the defining faces of Tumblr’s early years. At a time when the platform was shaping an entire visual language, her images appeared on countless mood boards, reposts, and fan pages, becoming inseparable from the era’s romantic, melancholic aesthetic.
What made Allison Harvard so influential wasn’t simply her appearance—it was the atmosphere she embodied. Somewhere between doll-like innocence and surreal beauty, she represented an internet culture that valued individuality over perfection. Her photographs felt less like fashion campaigns and more like fragments from another world.
As Tumblr’s influence spread into fashion, music, and photography, traces of Harvard’s aesthetic could suddenly be seen everywhere. Soft lighting, vintage lace, washed-out tones, oversized knitwear, and an almost cinematic sense of nostalgia became visual signatures of a generation.
More than a decade later, her images continue to circulate online, often without context, proof that some internet icons never really disappear. Trends may come and go, but Allison Harvard remains one of the defining faces of Tumblr’s golden era.

posts.alt_text:
There are influencers, and then there’s @madelineargy. Since 2021, the “Pretty Lonesome” host has made eight million strangers feel like her closest friends without even trying. Whether she’s giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it’s clear that Argy’s got range—and so does her closet.

Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

Tap the link in bio for our latest #ClosetTour to step inside her world, from the breakup that led to her most-treasured boots to why she’ll defend backpacks until the very end.

Photographer: @laaapavi
Makeup: @pircillapae (@aframe_agency)
Hair: @eduardomendezhair (@aframe_agency)
Editor-in-Chief: @faith_xue

posts.caption:
"Have you seen @erling Haaland's custom Birkins?" TikToker Jack Savoie said in a video that quickly went viral. "Baby, buckle up."
"OK the first thing I should say is that they're not all Birkins," @thesavoiedaily said in a phone interview with CNN.
Many of Haaland's bags are actually Haut à Courroies (HAC), the model that the Birkin is based off, but with a slightly taller and narrower profile. That doesn't do anything to dull the impact of Haaland's collection, though.
The enviable collection has triggered another round of discussion about men and the innocent handbag, with photos of Haaland on the tarmac posted to social media riddled with homophobia and comments questioning the striker's masculinity.
The specific irony of something like an Hermès HAC being categorized as hyperfeminine is that the bag was created in the late 19th century as a carryall for horse riding equipment, much like the rest of the brand's offerings at the time.
The brand wouldn't introduce a handbag specifically marketed towards women until many decades later, when it expanded its offerings beyond leather goods. "I think there's just an increasing appreciation for beautiful things, gender aside," Savoie said.
"I don't even know why these bags have genders."
📸 : Aitor Alcalde/UEFA/Getty Images/Jason Howard/Bauer-Griffin/GC Images/David Jensen/

posts.caption:
"In learning how to better recognize abundance, even in life's most uncertain moments, amidst challenge lay purpose and through change lay possibility, creating as an act of gratitude is my truest form of self-expression," wrote @marcjacobs in the show notes that accompanied his Spring 2027 runway presentation on Monday at the New York Public Library.
The blink-and-you'd-miss-it show (which lasted just four minutes) featured the designer's creative interpretation of the theme of gratitude. Sheer lamé dresses, hot pink croc mini skirts, sequin embroidered blazers and pantyhose in every color of the rainbow were all incorporated across the new collection. Swipe through for a peek at some of the standout looks.
📸: Rachel Tashjian/CNN; Dimitrios Kambouris/Getty Images

posts.caption:
Heatwaves are by nature an anomaly and the collections shown during fashion week are designed months in advance. But Europe is now the fastest-warming continent on Earth, heating up at a rate of twice the global average. The UN predicts the next 5 years will break even more temperature records.
Should designers, who work in an industry that is one of the biggest culprits for greenhouse gas emissions, at least be making clothes that people could reasonably aspire to wear in the hellfire summers to come? Or should we just submit to the heat-induced reverie?
Swipe to see looks from a sweltering Men’s Fashion Week in Paris, and read the full story at link in bio.

posts.caption:
Is this a handbag… or high jewellery?
With 4,732 gemstones totalling 423 carats, this one-of-a-kind clutch by the late Manuel Rosas of Rosior proves that exceptional craftsmanship doesn’t have to stay inside a jewellery box.
Crafted in silver and gold and set with diamonds, rubies, emeralds, sapphires, and amethysts, it’s a remarkable example of how jewellery techniques can transform an everyday object into collectible art.
Some creations aren’t defined by what they are.
They’re defined by how they’re made.
Would your clients see this as a handbag, a work of art, or a piece of high jewellery? Tell us in the comments.
Video credits - @treasurehousefair
#DiamondWorld #BeyondJewellery #HighJewellery #LuxuryCollectibles #JewelleryDesign

posts.alt_text:
Is this a handbag… or high jewellery?
With 4,732 gemstones totalling 423 carats, this one-of-a-kind clutch by the late Manuel Rosas of Rosior proves that exceptional craftsmanship doesn’t have to stay inside a jewellery box.

Crafted in silver and gold and set with diamonds, rubies, emeralds, sapphires, and amethysts, it’s a remarkable example of how jewellery techniques can transform an everyday object into collectible art.

Some creations aren’t defined by what they are.

They’re defined by how they’re made.

Would your clients see this as a handbag, a work of art, or a piece of high jewellery? Tell us in the comments.

Video credits - @treasurehousefair 

#DiamondWorld #BeyondJewellery #HighJewellery #LuxuryCollectibles #JewelleryDesign

posts.caption:
What if the biggest opportunity with amethyst isn’t the gemstone... but the way we sell it?
For decades, High Jewellery maisons have quietly demonstrated that luxury isn’t built on rarity alone.
It’s built on design.
While retail has often positioned amethyst as an “affordable coloured gemstone”, the world’s leading jewellery houses have consistently used it to create bold, artistic and highly collectible creations.
That’s an important distinction.
Because consumers rarely aspire to own something affordable.
They aspire to own something beautiful.
Perhaps the conversation around amethyst doesn’t need a new gemstone.
It needs a new narrative.
💬 How is amethyst positioned in your market today?
Save this post for your next buying discussion.
Share it with your team.
Let’s start the conversation.
#DiamondWorld #GemSphere #Amethyst #HighJewellery #ColoredGemstones

posts.caption:
Once considered heirloom accessories reserved for ceremonial dressing, diamond brooches are making a sophisticated comeback.
And this time, they are not just for grand occasions.
From blazers and sarees to handbags and hats, brooches are being embraced by a younger, style-conscious audience seeking jewellery that offers individuality, versatility, and design impact.
As modern consumers look for jewellery that adapts to multiple occasions and expresses personal style, brooches are moving from niche accessories to wardrobe essentials.
For jewellery retailers, the message is clear:
The diamond brooch is no longer an add-on. It is a category worth investing in.
Would your clients style a brooch on a blazer, sari, or handbag?
#DiamondWorld #DiamondBrooch #JewelleryTrends #BroochRevival #DWMagazine

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStars: Pride is not a June-only conversation. It is shifting beyond visibility and asking what comes next. What does it mean to build communities that outlast individuals? To create institutions instead of exceptions? To leave behind spaces where the next person does not have to start from scratch?
For ELLE’s Pride portfolio, designer James Ferreira (@jamesferreiralabel), drag king and theatre-maker Siaan (@mxsiaan), athlete Dutee Chand (@duteechand) and transmedia producer Zena Sagar (@zena.sagar)  reflect on the futures they are trying to build.
Their methods differ, but their ambition is remarkably similar: to leave behind a world more expansive than the one they inherited. Visibility may open the door, but it is community, institutions and shared power that keep it open.
Bringing these stories together is illustrator Vikalp Durga Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra), whose distinct visual language expands the portfolio into a shared queer world of colour, intimacy and imagination.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.
Editorial Director: Ainee Nizami Ahmedi (@aineenizamiahmedi)
Illustration: Vikalp Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra)
Words by : Tejaswi Subramanian (@tejnesss)
Cover Design: Shrushti Sudhakar (@shrushti_sudhakar)
Editorial Co-ordinator: Sneh Lad (@sneh_lad)
[Pride, Queer Rights]
posts.alt_text:
#ELLECoverStar: Victoria Harbour has been the face of Hong Kong for generations, where the city's soaring skyline meets the water in a view recognised around the world. It sets the stage for our latest cover shoot, with Jacqueline Fernandez (@jacquelienefernandez) bringing her unmistakable presence to one of the city's most iconic locations. As the skyline shifts with the light, she reminds us that the most captivating moments don't always ask for attention. Sometimes, they’re simply felt. Read our interview with the cover star at the link in bio.
.
On @jacquelienefernandez: Corset Octopus and Bell pants, both by @_demo_official. Crater ring and Wave amethyst ring, both by @figmenterie. Moo mules by @shopceecee___
.
Destination Partner: Hong Kong Tourism Board (@discoverhongkong)
.

posts.caption:
#ELLEOpinion: Portugal's loss to Spain filled our feeds with videos of fans crying, hugging strangers, and mourning a game that meant everything to them. No one questioned why they cared so much. No one told them to "touch grass". They were simply passionate, and emotional. And that's exactly the point. Whether it's football, Formula 1, K-pop, cricket or your favourite artist, fandom is built on the same emotions. We stay up until ungodly hours, buy merchandise, travel across countries, defend our favourites online, and celebrate victories as if they're our own. So, isn't it obvious that every fandom is cut from the same cloth? The difference isn't the intensity of the emotion, it's whose emotions society chooses to validate. Men's devotion to sport is celebrated as loyalty. Women's devotion to music is dismissed as obsession. Even women in sports fandoms are often forced to prove they're "real fans". Maybe it's time we stopped laughing at fangirls and started recognising the joy, community, and passion they bring. Head to the link in bio to read why K-pop and sports fandoms are far more alike than we'd like to admit.
Words by: @16ektasinha
[FIFA 2026, Portugal, Football Fans, Ronaldo]

posts.caption:
Cardi-D (for Devi) ready to bless us with that Ajanta-core couture! Never beating the fashion girl allegations!
Photos by @dirkalexanderphotography
posts.alt_text:
You know the Greek legend of Pygmalion, where a statue comes to life as a maiden? Well, at Rahul Mishra, the Indian version of the myth unfurled. Like as if by some couture divination, the carvings and statues, found in marvels like the Ajanta caves and Chennakesava Temple, took their first breaths and marched straight to sweltering hot Paris. 

YES, the “Devi” archetype is once again visited. But the Ajanta has been around for 2000 years and the reference can never be used ad nauseam. Nobody reserves that right to be inspired by it! And here it has been refreshingly done in forms of trompe-l'œil, appliqués, embellishments, costume jewellery, and embroidery, that distanced itself from being on-the-nose theatrical and felt rather clever.

The sculptural headpieces are made by Sumant Kumar and the hats are by the icon Stephen Jones. The blinding gems around the models were provided by Tanishq, which marks yet another year of their partnership. Indian story, Indian designer, Indian jewels. Well, it does take a whole country to make a couture show at such calibre 😇😇😇

posts.alt_text:
Taylor Swift married Travis Kelce on July 3rd at Madison Square Garden — We all know that. We also know that the bride wore Christian Dior Haute Couture Bridal by JW Anderson for the wedding. What we don’t know, however, is what the wedding gown looked like. 

This morning—this afternoon, if you’re in Paris—Dior held their Fall 2026 Couture Show featuring, as is the custom for these shows, a wedding gown as the final look. It was a whimsical and mostly traditional off-the-shoulder gown of plissé white chiffon layered underneath white netting which was decorated with fern-shaped lace, feathery fern appliques, and powdery white flowers like dandelions. Trailing the dress was an asymmetrical train of the same material. 

Being that Swift’s gown is one of the most anticipated pieces of bridal wear of the year (if not the decade) it’s almost impossible to imagine that Dior’s bridal look, shown just three days after the Swift/Kelce wedding, would have no relation or resemblance whatsoever to the gown Swift wore. Right?

Though details about the wedding are few, a source of The Hollywood Reporter described a luscious secret garden-esque environment which included “ferns and trees.” As well, AMC CEO Adam Aron revealed in now-deleted X posts that Swift wore a ”long white train” (like that seen on the runway today.) And, the flower appliqués on Dior’s wedding look are not dissimilar to the Queen Anne’s Lace seen on fragments of Swift’s bouquet which were posted by the guest who caught it during the toss.

All that said, Swift is notorious for dropping little hints and nods to her self-mythology. This is likely due to the voracious appetite of her fans who will dig into every detail of her lyrics and imagery for morsels of clarity about her inner life. Keeping her gown a secret seems to betray her career-long need to inflame speculation and conversation for as long as possible; Thus, the timing of Dior’s Couture Fall ‘26 show feels less-than coincidental as it provides yet another piece of the puzzle for fans to grab at. 

Or, are we totally overthinking it? Is this just the Swiftie spell working its magic on us?

posts.caption:
Welp, America 250, a once-in-a-generation celebration, turned out to be the most on-brand shitshow for the Trump administration.
The nation’s semiquincentennial should have been an easy win for the president, especially since planning had been reportedly underway for nearly a decade. Organizers blamed the weather for the sparse crowds and canceled events, but the vibes were off way before the forecast.
Funding has come under scrutiny after House Dems alleged that some donors were unknowingly redirected from the bipartisan America 250 nonprofit to a different nonprofit promoting Trump-backed events. At least 10 states declined to send official delegations over concerns about the event’s messaging, including North Carolina, which instead ended up represented by a booth featuring images of the Confederate flag.
The weekend’s laundry list of L’s included masked white nationalists marching to “reclaim” America, MAGA supporters arguing with the National Guard after being ordered to evacuate for severe weather, and many taking refuge inside the National Museum of African American History and Culture (LOL, the irony). There were also baptisms on the National Mall?? Interesting choice for a nation founded on separating church and state.
As for entertainment, Vanilla Ice, the last recognizable headliner, bailed after weather delayed his set, leaving attendees with a band attempting to cover Beyoncé. Yes, Beyoncé. Later, members of the U.S. Armed Forces sang a rendition of Earth, Wind & Fire’s “September,” a song the band has previously objected to Trump using.
Saturday ended with what Trump called the “best fireworks show EVER,” promoted as a potential Guinness World Records contender. Instead, the display briefly gave D.C. the worst air quality of any major city on Earth per CNN, prompting a Code Red air quality alert.
Happy birthday, America?
#independenceday #fourthofjuly #dc #reels #news

posts.caption:
Paris was the first city to host a brick-and-mortar Shein store, which closed recently after being boycotted, and now the French Parliament has passed a law which will hamper the growth of ultra-fast fashion retailers in the country.
The law targets Asian e-commerce retailers like Shein (the law was reportedly nicknamed the “anti-Shein law,) and will impose fees on products based on a set of criteria designed to define and grade ultra-fast fashion. That criteria looks at the breadth of the retailers’ product assortment, the speed of new releases, and the “repairability” of garments. IE, if a company is churning out loads of cheap garments that are more costly to repair than to replace, their products will be subjected to fees.
Further, advertising will be restricted, even with regard to social media influencers. Lawmakers likened these measures to those imposed on cigarettes and alcohol, which seems an apt comparison; it is widely understood that products known to be harmful to the public shouldn’t be advertised with reckless abandon. Not unlike cigarettes, ultra-fast fashion produces significant greenhouse gas emissions and have been found to be produced with materials that are harmful to the body.
But, is there a snag in the logic? It’s important to note that the law is careful to differentiate ultra-fast fashion and just plain-old fast fashion. European-owned companies like Zara and H&M were spared, with some environmental groups and lawmakers criticizing the law, saying that it had been “watered down” to forgive the business practices of European-owned retailers. But the centrist members of parliament who introduced the bill argue that France must “start somewhere” in the fight against fast fashion.
Which, fair point. The whole planet has to start somewhere. With this new law, France is still a pioneer in pushing back against ultra-fast fashion. They’re hardly the only nation for whom fashion is big business, so here’s hoping more change is on the horizon.

posts.caption:
One moment, he was breaking through. The next, he was everywhere. Our July cover star, AP Dhillon (@apdhillon), has become one of the defining voices of a generation. Through the rise, the noise and the spotlight, he’s remained focused on what matters most: the music. In our latest cover story, he opens up about balancing the artist the world knows with the person he’s always been.

posts.alt_text:
He may be AP (@apdhillon) to the world, but he’s also Amritpal Singh Dhillon. The man who still goes to the same barber, skips a hair and grooming team because he believes imperfection is real, and often forgets just how famous he is. Fame brought him freedom, financial stability, and love from fans that money can’t buy—but it also came at a cost. Our July cover star AP Dhillon (@apdhillon) opens up about life beyond the spotlight.

Chairperson: Avarna Jain (@avarna_jain) 
CEO: Debashish Ghosh

posts.caption:
#EsquireHotlist: This July, as the monsoon settles in and outdoor plans get rained out, India’s museums and galleries offer a better alternative: air-conditioned spaces worth the trip. From New Delhi and Mumbai to Patna, galleries are staging shows that trace the country’s modernist inheritance. Some are closing-soon retrospectives worth catching before the season turns; others are just getting started, proving that the monsoon isn’t a dead season for art, just an indoor one.
Written by: Rudra Mulmule (@poetryandpasta)
#EsquireIndia #ArtShows #ArtExhibitions

posts.caption:
Earlier days when Sachin Tendulkar used to get out early, Everyone used to switch off their T.V considering that India had lost the match there itself. But when this man arrived, He changed the whole scenario of The Indian Cricket Team. After that even if Sachin gets out early, People didn't switch off their T.V because they knew that this man is yet to come on the field and the moment he arrived on the crease, From then it completely changed the game of Cricket and won matches for the team and gave hopes to each and every Indian person. And the time he took the responsibility of being the captain of The Indian Cricket Team without thinking that it might affect his batting as well where other captains stepped down, Not only he created history in Our Country but in The World by winning 3 Major ICC Trophies under his leadership in less than 10 years than any other team and The Captain to win 5 Trophies in IPL from 2008 to 2023 for CSK Team.
Wishing a very Happy Birthday to 'The Greatest Indian Captain of All Time', The Best Wicket-Keeper, And The Best Finisher ever, ICC Hall of Fame Inductee, The Strawberry and Aura Farmer of Ranchi, Cake Paglu and also the CEO of the Number 7 for a Reason 😎☝🏻, Lt. Colonel Mahendra Singh Dhoni 💙💙💛❤️🥰🙌🏻🏏💯
Note : Yeah I know Rohit Sharma has also won a T20 World Cup and Champions Trophy and Suryakumar Trophy has won T20 World Cup as well, And I also believe that India will keep winning the World Cup in Future no matter who the captain is, Because these players are representing our country and they should be respected equally unlike some Chhapri Illiterate Nibbas which are also known as Lockdown Kids and some Toxic as well as trying to be Oversmart Fans.

posts.caption:
Paris Haute Couture Week is officially underway, and Day 1 delivered everything couture promises: impeccable craftsmanship and fashion that felt closer to art than clothing.
From Schiaparelli’s surreal The Call of the Void to Dior’s artistic tribute inspired by Lynda Benglis, and Rahul Mishra’s Devi: The Eternal Muse, rooted in the beauty of ancient Indian sculpture, the opening day celebrated creativity without limits.
Add a star-studded front row featuring Priyanka Chopra Jonas, Sabrina Carpenter, Bad Bunny, Mingyu, and Cardi B, and Paris set the tone for an unforgettable couture season.
#ParisHauteCoutureWeek #HauteCouture #Schiaparelli #Dior #RahulMishra

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Norman Jean Roy @normanjeanroy
Stylist: Law Roach @luxurylaw
Writer: Kayla Webley Adler @kaylaw
Hair: Orlando Pita at Orlo Salon @hairbyorlandopita @orlosalon
Makeup: Fulvia Farolfi at MA+ Group @fulviafarolfi @maworldgroup
Manicure: Deborah Lippmann for Deborah Lippmann @thelippmannofficial @thedeborahlippmann
Set design: Marla Weinhoff at 11th House Agency @marlaweinhoff @11thhouseagency
Produced by Alexey Galetskiy Productions @alexeyg

Sure, flip-flops are an obvious choice for pool- or beachside moments, but more than that, they can work with denim, dresses, and even workwear—that is, when you know what you’re doing. A true summertime style savior, if you will!
At the link in bio, find seven chic flip-flop outfits from street style stars and celebrities like @ayoddebiri and @roses_are_rosie.

posts.caption:
New @chanelofficial is finally here! @matthieu_blazy just showed his haute couture fall/winter 2026 collection at the Grand Palais in Paris, in front of guests like Tilda Swinton, @lupitanyongo, @pascalispunk, and more—all outfitted in the buzzy designer’s effervescent Chanel creations, of course. Aptly titled “Gaby and the Beanstalk,” Blazy’s latest cast the legendary founder of the maison as the heroine of her own imagined fairy tale, complete with deconstructed tweed skirt suits, sheer silhouettes, along with thorny vines and spiky botanical motifs.
“Fairy tales are there to make you uncomfortable,” Blazy said backstage after the show. “‘Am I going to come out of this book alive or not?’ I liked this idea of a dangerous flower—we call them the toxic flowers of Gabrielle.”
Read the full review at the link in bio.

posts.caption: My outfit definitely expresses how I’m feeling. I might get extra done up if I’m feeling bad or in a funk and I don’t feel good when I’m not put together. Also I love style and fashion it’s like art for me putting outfits together. But there are many people that just get dressed and don’t think about it one way or another so I’m not sure that it affects everyone in the same way.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text:
💕💚After I went on Lorraine last year I made a silent promise to myself that I would be back at the ITV studios and I had my eyes set on @thismorning. I can’t believe just a few months later, this promise became a reality.

Of course I was low key stressing 😅😅 but there was absolutely zero reason to be nervous because the entire (and I’m not exaggerating) team were so warm and lovely that I felt like I was hanging with friends all day. 

Firstly thank you for everyone that tuned in and sent good luck vibes🍀💖! A huge shout out to @lilaflintroberts @sarah_barlow  and @amberstyledit for the opportunity to shine a light on #fashionpsychology and for allowing me to flex my styling skills. 

💃🏾🔥I couldn’t have asked for better models to showcase those amazing looks. @kate_mitchell78 @jazzifizzle53 and @misskspence you KILLT IT 

And last but certainly not least the pros @hollywilloughby and @alisonhammond55 thank you for making me feel so comfortable, it was an honour to watch you both work! 

Stay tuned for a reel on all of the BTS goss but for now I’m in Estonia about to speak on the biggest stage I have so far. Wish me luck! 🍀💖 

Dress: @fumithelabel 
Shoes: @asos 

#Tv #fashionpsychology #press #itv #thismorning

posts.caption:
We’ve all heard the advice to be present.
But fashion psychology suggests there may be one exception: shopping.
Research has found that people who think about and value their future selves tend to make wiser spending decisions, choosing purchases that align with what’s genuinely important to them over the long term rather than what simply feels exciting in the moment. The key isn’t ignoring the present. It’s making sure your present-day choices also serve the person you’ll become.
I was reminded of this at a brilliant event hosted by @wolfandbadger in partnership with @sign_ofthetimes , where founder @antoniajohnstone shared valuable insights on buying and selling investment pieces.
One thing that really stuck with me is that an investment piece doesn’t have to be luxury.
It’s anything that continues to earn its place in your wardrobe years later.
So before your next purchase, try asking yourself one question:
Will Future Me still be grateful I bought this?
Outfit deets:
Top: @mejimeji.co
Skirt: @asos
Shoes: @simplybeuk
Earrings: @bettyandbiddy
Bag: @telfarglobal
Reference:
Bartels, D. M., & Urminsky, O. (2015). *To Know and to Care: How Awareness and Valuation of the Future Jointly Shape Consumer Spending*. *Journal of Consumer Research, 41*(6), 1469-1485. https://doi.org/10.1086/680670
#shopping #fashionpsychology #luxury #psychology

For nearly two decades, our cover star, Yami Gautam Dhar, has built a career on one simple principle: never be predictable. posts.caption:
“You know, there comes a time at a very crucial point in your life when you become absolutely fearless and just want to work without having anything else on your mind. The only thing I think about is whether I will love doing what I’ve just committed to. Of course, you want the outcome to be proportionate to your passion, but I also know it is not in my hands. What is in my control is to make sure that whatever I say ‘yes’ to, I’m happy to do, to enjoy it thoroughly.”

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
Gap has gotten in a bit of trouble for rereleasing a collection from the 2000s. Any guesses what the drama is about? 🤔⁠
⁠
Turns out, the brand’s rerelease pivots garments from cotton to polyester—and people are calling the brand out, for using the material and for creating clothes in the style of ultra fast fashion brands like SHEIN and Temu 😶‍🌫️⁠
⁠
These critiques are correct, but there’s something else that seems to be missing: Gap is a fast fashion brand. While designer collaborations and stunts like this build buzz, they’re closer to SHEIN than one might think.⁠
⁠
Swipe to explore the reaction, how Gap rates, and alternatives to patterned clothing from “Good” and “Great” brands. What are your thoughts on this? Share in the comments, then visit the link for recommendations of brands that embrace patterns ↑⁠
⁠
📸 Zac Posen⁠
⁠
#Gap #GapStudio #Shopping #SummerStyles #Sustainability

Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠

posts.caption:
Where do high street brands fall as far as availability and ethics? We mapped them, to help visualize the solution 🤓⁠
⁠
In plotting brands by how well or poorly they rate by our ratings methodology and how available they are, the results made clear something we talk and talk and talk about: smaller brands are more likely to be more sustainable, by taking on measures like adopting circularity initiatives, committing to lower impact fibres, and paying workers fairly.⁠
⁠
Look to the top left as proof, where so many “Good” rated but less-available brands (Sweden’s @asket, Australia’s @spell, France’s @veja, the US’ @reformation) land in comparison to the bottom right, where brands that rate our three lowest of “It’s a Start” through “We Avoid” land: they're all mega-corporations with global availability (adidas, Nike, MAC, H&M, Lacoste, Zara, SHEIN), brands that are profiting the most but doing the least.⁠
⁠
At the other end, smaller and lower rated brands (Malin+Goetz, Sézane, J.Crew) show that there’s always room to grow at any size while bigger and higher rated brands (Aesop, Lush, The North Face) illustrate how change *is* possible.⁠
⁠
Note the lone “Great”—our highest rating—is @nudiejeans, which has remarkable ethics and availability. That’s what brands should strive for, and is proof brands can work better for people, the planet, and animals should they commit to working transparently 💚⁠
⁠
Where would your favourite brand fall on this grid? Which should we map next? Share in the comments, then visit the link to explore brand ratings at the link ↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #Shopping #SustainableFashion #Sustainability #FashionTech
posts.alt_text:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus.
A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ’s @zachbaron, “Every single location on this movie would’ve been the hardest location on any other movie I’ve ever done… Every time we’d go somewhere, we’d be like, ‘Well, Iceland will be easier.’ And then it’s raining sideways and it’s fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‘Yeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.’”
For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: “All of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren’t difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.”
Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana
Tailoring by Yelena Travkina and Susanna Badalyan
Key Gaffer by @mick411
Key Grip by @clayton_n_iii
Retouching by @bermudezstudio
Modeling by Kara Smatt
Produced by @campproductions
posts.alt_text:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Robert Pattinson as Antinous. 

Pattinson’s part in The Odyssey is not big—he plays the cockiest of the many suitors who have installed themselves at Ithaca, seeking the hand of Penelope (played by Anne Hathaway) while Matt Damon’s Odysseus is away. “My character’s a little like James Woods in Casino. That was my inspiration for it,” Pattinson said. “He’s like a little sleazy. I kept saying at my costume fitting, ‘I really want to have leopard underpants.’”

Pattinson talks to GQ’s @zachbaron about teaming up with Nolan again for The Odyssey, alongside co-stars Matt Damon and Tom Holland, at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Tom Holland as Telemachus.
When Christopher Nolan first offered Holland a part in The Odyssey, the production dates were identical to those of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (and Holland’s commitments to the Spider-Man franchise are ironclad). “I said to Chris, like, ‘Look, I want to do this movie, but if I’m going to do it, I’m going to have to call Sony and have a very uncomfortable conversation,’ ” Holland said. Then he immediately had a very uncomfortable conversation.
“Chris has that reputation of ‘This movie isn’t going to go five months over, and we aren’t actually going to lose Tom for two years,’ ” Holland said. “Any other director, it might have been a slightly different conversation.” The Odyssey started on the day Nolan said it would and finished nine days early.
For our summer cover story, Holland talks about coming of age on set, getting to star alongside his partner on another project, and how he’s approaching the future of his career at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Some games are remembered for who lifts the trophy. Others for who walks away ⚽
In the span of two days, #Brazil crashed out in the Round of 16, prompting #NeymarJr. to retire from international football. Portugal followed with a Round of 16 defeat to Spain, bringing #CristianoRonaldo's final #FIFAWorldCup campaign to an end, just as he had announced before the tournament.
For nearly two decades, Ronaldo and Neymar have been two of #football's biggest names. Between them, they've featured in nine #FIFAWorldCups, broken countless records and given fans moments they'll never forget ⭐
#WorldCup2026 signals the end of an iconic generation. With Cristiano Ronaldo's final World Cup behind him and Neymar Jr. stepping away from international football, the sport turns the page on one of its defining eras and into a new one.
📸: Getty
✍️: Nishu Verma (@nishooverma)

posts.caption:
#TaylorSwift and #TravisKelce's wedding hijacked my birthday. Between slices of cake, I found myself refreshing live blogs and zooming into grainy guest lists. Two days later, I was reading out updates from #AamirKhan and Gauri Spratt's intimate wedding to my family with the urgency of someone who'd actually made the guest list.
That's when it hit me. #CelebrityWeddings have a strange way of blurring the line between spectator and participant. We celebrate them like personal milestones, dissect every detail and keep following the story long after the vows are exchanged.
Why do weddings, more than almost any other celebrity milestone, inspire this kind of collective investment? I explore the psychology behind our parasocial relationships, the aspirational fantasy celebrity weddings sell, and why social media has transformed them into one of the internet's favourite cultural events.
Tap the 🔗 in bio to read more
✍️ Sanjana Ray (@sanjanaray03)

posts.caption:
For his second couture collection, #MatthieuBlazy took his audience out of the enormous great hall of the Grand Palais and into a small salon and a fairytale set sprawling with giant vines and exotic flowers - bringing the clothes up close and personal.
And what clothes! No costume here, these were clothes to live in - even sit in! That’s not to say some weren’t encrusted with pearls, gems, fabric florals in their gezillians… but just as many were drop-dead simple.
In such a short time, Matthieu Blazy has rewritten the rules at @chanelofficial. From his all-age model casting to those two tone shoes that sent everyone doolally last season and, as guest Dina Asher Smith put it: ‘The pure joy of clothes.’
